vulnerabilities.description: Use of innerHTML with user-controlled input. While currently limited to emoji literals, innerHTML should be avoided in favor of textContent for consistency and to prevent future XSS risks if logic changes.

User-supplied todo text is interpolated into aria-label attributes without explicit sanitization (e.g., Excluir ${todo.text}). While setAttribute is safer than innerHTML, special characters in todo.text could potentially cause attribute injection. Should use textContent for aria-label or sanitize input.
Same as line 172: user-supplied todo text in aria-label template string (Editar tarefa: ${todo.text}) without explicit sanitization.

Non-atomic file writes using writeFileSync. Race condition vulnerability if multiple processes write concurrently; file can be corrupted mid-write. Session.js fixed this with atomic writes (#2307) but memory.js still uses non-atomic approach.
No explicit JSON size validation before parsing localStorage data. Malformed or oversized data could cause performance issues, though error handling exists.

recommendations: Replace innerHTML usage (app.js:154) with textContent: `checkBtn.textContent = todo.completed ? '✅' : '';`
Sanitize or validate aria-label attributes by using textContent assignment instead of setAttribute with template strings, or implement a helper function that escapes special characters
Implement atomic writes in memory.js similar to session.js using temp file + rename pattern to prevent data corruption under concurrent writes
Add explicit input length validation for todo text (e.g., max 1000 chars) to prevent localStorage quota exhaustion
Consider adding Content-Security-Policy headers if this is served over HTTP/HTTPS
Add input type validation in loadTodos() to verify todo object structure before using them
Implement localStorage quota checking before saving to handle edge cases gracefully
